title: Indian Standard Time

infobox.value: (**IST**), sometimes also called **India Standard Time**, is the [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") observed throughout the [Republic of India](/wiki/Republic_of_India "Republic of India"), with a [time offset](/wiki/Time_offset "Time offset") of [UTC+05:30](/wiki/UTC%2B05:30 "UTC+05:30"). India does not observe [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") or other seasonal adjustments. In [military and aviation time](/wiki/List_of_military_time_zones "List of military time zones"), IST is designated **E\*** ("Echo-Star").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is indicated as *Asia/Kolkata* in the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/Tz_database "Tz database").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Time zone observed in India; UTC+05:30

For Irish Standard Time, see [Time in the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Time_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Time in the Republic of Ireland").

| Indian Standard Time | |
| --- | --- |
| [Time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") | |
| Indian Standard Time | |
| UTC offset | |
| IST | [UTC+05:30](/wiki/UTC%2B05:30 "UTC+05:30") |
| Current time | |
| 23:54, 26 March 2026 IST [[refresh](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=purge)] | |
| Observance of [DST](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") | |
| DST is not observed in this time zone. | |

**Indian Standard Time** (**IST**), sometimes also called **India Standard Time**, is the [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") observed throughout the [Republic of India](/wiki/Republic_of_India "Republic of India"), with a [time offset](/wiki/Time_offset "Time offset") of [UTC+05:30](/wiki/UTC%2B05:30 "UTC+05:30"). India does not observe [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") or other seasonal adjustments. In [military and aviation time](/wiki/List_of_military_time_zones "List of military time zones"), IST is designated **E\*** ("Echo-Star").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is indicated as *Asia/Kolkata* in the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/Tz_database "Tz database").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Time in India](/wiki/Time_in_India "Time in India")

The Indian Standard Time was adopted on 1 January 1906 during the [British era](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") with the phasing out of its precursor [Madras Time](/wiki/Madras_Time "Madras Time") (Railway Time),[[2]](#cite_note-2) and after [Independence in 1947](/wiki/Indian_Independence_movement "Indian Independence movement"), the [Union government](/wiki/Union_government "Union government") established IST as the official time for the whole country, although [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") and [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") retained their own local time (known as [Calcutta Time](/wiki/Calcutta_Time "Calcutta Time") and [Bombay Time](/wiki/Bombay_Time "Bombay Time")) until 1948 and 1955, respectively.[[3]](#cite_note-Princely_states-3) The Central observatory was moved from [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") to a location at Shankargarh Fort in [Allahabad district](/wiki/Allahabad_district "Allahabad district"), so that it would be as close to [UTC+05:30](/wiki/UTC%2B05:30 "UTC+05:30") as possible.[[4]](#cite_note-Hans_2018-4)

Daylight Saving Time (DST) was used briefly during the [China–India War of 1962](/wiki/Sino-Indian_War "Sino-Indian War") and the Indo-Pakistani [Wars of 1965](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1965") and [1971](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971").[[5]](#cite_note-timez-5)

### Former timezones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Former timezones")]

| Time Zone | UTC | Period | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Bombay Time](/wiki/Bombay_Time "Bombay Time") | [UTC+04:51](/wiki/UTC%2B04:51 "UTC+04:51") | 1884–1955 | Official |
| [Calcutta Time](/wiki/Calcutta_Time "Calcutta Time") | [UTC+05:53:20](/wiki/UTC%2B05:30 "UTC+05:30") | 1884–1948 | Official |
| [Madras Time](/wiki/Madras_Time "Madras Time") | [UTC+05:21:14](/wiki/UTC%2B05:00 "UTC+05:00") | 1802–1906 | Railways |

## Calculation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Calculation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/IST-Mirzapur.svg/250px-IST-Mirzapur.svg.png)](/wiki/File:IST-Mirzapur.svg)

Location of Mirzapur (near [Prayagraj](/wiki/Prayagraj "Prayagraj")) and the 82°30’ E longitude that is used as the reference longitude for IST

Indian Standard Time is calculated from the reference longitude of IST at 82°30'E passing near [Vindhyachal](/wiki/Vindhyachal "Vindhyachal") of [Mirzapur district](/wiki/Mirzapur_district "Mirzapur district") in Uttar Pradesh.[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6) In 1905, the meridian passing east of Allahabad was declared as a standard time zone for [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") and was declared as IST in 1947 for the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India").[[7]](#cite_note-7) This longitude of 82°30'E was chosen as the [standard meridian](/wiki/Standard_meridian "Standard meridian") for the whole country[[8]](#cite_note-8) as it is located centrally between [western India](/wiki/Western_India "Western India") ([local time UTC +05:00](/wiki/UTC%2B05:00 "UTC+05:00")) and [northeastern India](/wiki/Northeastern_India "Northeastern India") ([local time UTC +06:00](/wiki/UTC%2B06:00 "UTC+06:00")). Currently, the [National Physical Laboratory of India](/wiki/National_Physical_Laboratory_of_India "National Physical Laboratory of India") maintains the Indian Standard Time with the help of the [Allahabad Observatory](/wiki/Allahabad_Observatory "Allahabad Observatory").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Criticisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criticisms")]

The country's east–west distance of more than 2,933 kilometres (1,822 mi) covers over 29° of longitude, resulting in the sun rising and setting almost two hours earlier on India's [eastern](/wiki/Anjaw_district "Anjaw district") border than in the [Rann of Kutch](/wiki/Rann_of_Kutch "Rann of Kutch") in the far west. Inhabitants of the [northeastern states](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") have to advance their clocks with the early sunrise to avoid the extra consumption of energy after daylight hours.[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6)

In the late 1980s, a team of researchers proposed separating the country into two or three time zones to conserve energy. The binary system that they suggested involved a return to British-era time zones, but the recommendations were not adopted.[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6)[[10]](#cite_note-thehindu2002-10)

In 2001, the government established a four-member committee under the [Ministry of Science and Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Science_and_Technology_(India) "Ministry of Science and Technology (India)") to examine the need for multiple time zones and daylight saving.[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6) The findings of the committee, which were presented to [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India") in 2004 by the Minister of Science and Technology, [Kapil Sibal](/wiki/Kapil_Sibal "Kapil Sibal"), did not recommend changes to the unified system, stating that 'the prime meridian was chosen with reference to a central station, and that the expanse of the Indian State was not large.'[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Though the government has consistently refused to split the country into multiple time zones, provisions in labour laws such as the *Plantations Labour Act, 1951* allow the union and state governments to define and set the local time for a particular industrial area.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), tea gardens follow a separate time zone, known as the *Chaibagan* or *Bagan* time ('Tea Garden Time'), which is one hour ahead of IST.[[13]](#cite_note-HindustanTimes2008-13) Still, Indian Standard Time remains the only officially used time.

In 2014, Chief Minister of Assam [Tarun Gogoi](/wiki/Tarun_Gogoi "Tarun Gogoi") started campaigning for another time zone for [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and other [northeastern states](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") of India.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, the proposal would need to be cleared by the [union government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India").

In June 2017, the [Department of Science and Technology](/wiki/Department_of_Science_and_Technology_(India) "Department of Science and Technology (India)") (DST) indicated that it is once again studying the feasibility of two time zones for India. Proposals for creating an additional Eastern India Time (EIT at [UTC+06:00](/wiki/UTC%2B06:00 "UTC+06:00")), shifting default IST to [UTC+05:00](/wiki/UTC%2B05:00 "UTC+05:00") and daylight saving (Indian Daylight Time for IST and Eastern India Daylight Time for EIT) starting on 14 April ([Ambedkar Jayanti](/wiki/Ambedkar_Jayanti "Ambedkar Jayanti")) and ending on 2 October ([Gandhi Jayanti](/wiki/Gandhi_Jayanti "Gandhi Jayanti")) was submitted to DST for consideration.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Time signals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Standard_Time&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Time signals")]

Official time signals are generated by the [Time and Frequency Standards Laboratory](/wiki/Time_and_Frequency_Standards_Laboratory "Time and Frequency Standards Laboratory") at the National Physical Laboratory in [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), for both commercial and official use. The signals are based on [atomic clocks](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") and synchronised with the worldwide system of clocks that support [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time").

Features of the Time and Frequency Standards Laboratory include:

* [High-frequency](/wiki/High_frequency "High frequency") broadcast service operating at 10 MHz under call sign *ATA* to synchronise the user clock within a millisecond;
* [Indian National Satellite System](/wiki/Indian_National_Satellite_System "Indian National Satellite System") satellite-based standard time and frequency broadcast service, which offers IST correct to ±10 microsecond and frequency calibration of up to ±10−10.
* Time and frequency calibrations made with the help of [pico-](/wiki/Picosecond "Picosecond") and [nanoseconds](/wiki/Nanosecond "Nanosecond") time interval [frequency counters](/wiki/Frequency_counter "Frequency counter") and [phase](/wiki/Phase_(waves) "Phase (waves)") recorders.

IST is taken as the standard time as it passes through almost the centre of India. To communicate the exact time to the people, the exact time is broadcast over the national [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio") and [Doordarshan](/wiki/Doordarshan "Doordarshan") television network. Telephone companies have dedicated phone numbers connected to mirror [time servers](/wiki/Time_server "Time server") that also relay the precise time. Another increasingly popular means of obtaining the time is through [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) receivers.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As part of the **Times Dissemination Project**, which is overseen by the [Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food, and Public Distribution](/wiki/Ministry_of_Consumer_Affairs,_Food_and_Public_Distribution "Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution"), [NavIC](/wiki/NavIC "NavIC") will take the position of GPS as the reference time provider at the National Physical Laboratory from 2025. With an atomic clock in each of the four other centres—[Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad"), [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Bhubaneswar](/wiki/Bhubaneswar "Bhubaneswar"), and [Guwahati](/wiki/Guwahati "Guwahati")—the reference time from NavIC will be transmitted via an [optical fibre](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber") link from the Faridabad centre. Indian Standard Time must be used as the exclusive time reference for official, commercial, administrative, and legal documents, according to draft regulations published by the [Department of Consumer Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Consumer_Affairs_(India) "Department of Consumer Affairs (India)"). Exceptions will be permitted for astronomy, navigation, and scientific research.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)
